segments.p2-5-g54: "И потому" is a connective: И = 'and' linking to the previous thought and потому = 'for that reason/therefore', so together they mean 'and therefore' or 'so'. Я is the nominative pronoun 'I'. Должен is a predicative adjective with modal force ('ought to / must'), agreeing with the implied subject (masculine); дать is an infinitive expressing the action required; Зверкову is the dative of the proper name Zverkov indicating the person who will receive the action; пощечину is the accusative singular of пощёчина, 'a slap' (a single blow to the cheek).
segments.p2-5-g55: Я = 'I'. Обязан is a short-form adjective/predicative meaning 'obliged' or 'compelled' (it functions like a modal: 'I am obliged / must'). Дать is the infinitive 'to give' and here the object is omitted because it is understood from context (the slap mentioned earlier).
segments.p2-5-g56: Итак is a discourse adverb meaning 'so/thus/now then' that signals a conclusion or decision based on what came before; here it opens the statement that follows.
segments.p2-5-g57: Решено is the short/form past-participle used impersonally to mean 'it's decided' or 'resolved' (resultant state), often with a semicolon or pause after it in speech.
segments.p2-5-g58: Я = 'I'. Лечу is the present tense of лететь literally 'I fly', used colloquially/metaphorically here as 'I'm rushing / I'm off' (not literal flight). Теперь = 'now'. Дать + ему + пощечину is an infinitival purpose/intent clause: 'to give him a slap' — giving the action (дать) and its indirect object (ему, 'to him') and direct object (пощечину, 'a slap'). The whole clause means 'I'm off now to give him a slap.'
segments.p2-5-g59: Погоняй is an imperative form (2nd-person singular or a colloquial command) from the verb meaning 'drive' or 'spur on' (used toward a rider/driver), so here it is a shouted command to urge on the animals or driver.
segments.p2-5-g40: A one-word exclamation. Нет is the simple negation/interjection used as a full utterance meaning 'No!' here; it has no explicit subject or verb and stands alone as a response or rejection.
segments.p2-5-g41: Надо is an impersonal modal expression meaning 'it is necessary' or 'one must,' and it governs an infinitive without an explicit subject. Много is an adverbial/quantifier modifying the infinitive that follows; сделать is the perfective infinitive 'to do/complete' and serves as the verbal core of this impersonal clause.
segments.p2-5-g42: Чтоб (colloquial form of чтобы) introduces a purpose/result clause and here takes the infinitive выкупить; всё это is the direct-object phrase ('all this') placed before the infinitive for emphasis. Выкупить is the perfective infinitive 'to redeem/buy back' used figuratively as 'make amends for / redeem (oneself)'.
segments.p2-5-g43: Прокричал is the past tense, perfective verb 'shouted out' describing a completed action; in narrative the subject pronoun я follows the verb (inverted order) and simply restates the speaker. The punctuation sets this as the reported shouted utterance.
segments.p2-5-g44: Но is the coordinating conjunction 'but/however' introducing the speaker's declaration. Выкуплю is the first-person singular future of the perfective verb 'выкупить' ('I will redeem/buy back'), followed by the disjunctive или ('or') linking the alternative clause. В эту же ночь is a temporal phrase: в + accusative (эту ночь) meaning 'in/on this very night,' with же as an emphatic particle meaning 'very/just'; погибну is first-person singular future ('I will perish'), and на месте ('on the spot') is an adverbial locative phrase specifying immediate location.
segments.p2-5-g45: Пошел is the perfective verb form (past tense) of пойти, often used as a short exclamation meaning 'Go!' or 'Off you go!' in colloquial speech; here it functions as an imperative-like interjection signaling the start of motion. It is not a full sentence with explicit subject—its force is pragmatic.
segments.p2-5-g15: Пусть is an optative/jussive particle used here as a standalone imperative meaning 'let it be' or 'so be it'. It does not inflect for person or number and often functions as a terse command or resignation when exclaimed alone.
segments.p2-5-g16: Крикнул is the perfective past tense, masculine singular form of крикнуть ('to shout'); the following pronoun я is the subject. The inverted order 'крикнул я' is stylistic and emphasizes the act of shouting; the comma marks the end of the main clause.
segments.p2-5-g17: Отвечая is a present adverbial participle (деепричастие) formed from отвечать, indicating a simultaneous or attendant action ('while answering'). Себе is the dative reflexive pronoun meaning 'to myself' (here 'answering to myself'). Together they mark that the shouting occurred while he was answering himself.
segments.p2-5-g18: Теперь is an adverb meaning 'now' or 'at this moment'; ведь is a particle adding 'after all' or 'you know' (emphatic/explanatory); уж is an emphatic particle meaning 'already' or 'indeed' here; всё is the neuter pronoun 'everything' serving as the subject; погибло is the perfective past neuter singular of погибнуть ('to perish/be ruined'). The sequence of particles (теперь ведь уж) stacks emphasis before the subject and predicate in this exclamatory sentence.
segments.p2-5-g19: Их is the possessive pronoun 'their' (used before the noun to mark possession). Уж is the particle 'already' (emphatic). И here functions as an intensifier ('even') in the phrase уж и. След is a masculine noun 'trail/track', and простыл is the past masculine singular of простыть used metaphorically here ('turned cold'/'grew cold') to say the trail disappeared or became cold. The clause literally says 'Their trail had already grown cold.'
segments.p2-5-g20: Но is the coordinating conjunction 'but/however'. Всё равно is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'still' or 'all the same'/'anyway' (всё here is 'everything' and равно is from 'equal', forming the frozen expression conveying lack of difference or that something remains so despite the prior clause). The colon after равно introduces what he knew despite that.
segments.p2-5-g21: Я is the subject pronoun 'I'. Знал is the masculine past tense of знать ('to know') and here simply reports that he was aware/aware of the fact in the past: 'I knew.' The comma separates this reporting clause from the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.p2-5-g22: Куда is an interrogative/adverbial pronoun used here in an indirect question meaning 'where (to)'. Они is the pronoun 'they' (subject of the subordinate clause). Поехали is the past plural of поехать ('to set off/leave by transport'); together 'куда они поехали' is an indirect question 'where they had gone.'
segments.p2-5-g65: Просто is an adverb ('simply/merely') that modifies the whole clause, giving a tone of blunt resolve. Войду is the 1st person singular future of войти (perfective) meaning 'I will enter' — perfective marks a single completed action; it comes before дам, and the conjunction и links two coordinated verbs with the same subject. Дам is the 1st person singular future of дать; here in colloquial context it means 'I'll give him one / I'll hit him' (i.e. deliver a blow), not a literal gift.
segments.p2-5-g66: Они 'they' is the subject; все ('all') emphasizes every one of them. Будут сидеть is a periphrastic future: the auxiliary будут (future of быть) plus the imperfective infinitive сидеть expresses an ongoing/continuous action in the future — 'will be sitting'. В зале is a prepositional phrase ('in the hall') marking location.
segments.p2-5-g67: А is a contrastive conjunction here ('and/while' or 'but') linking the contrasting clause about 'he'. Он 'he' is the subject; the verb быть is omitted (understood from the previous clause), so на диване с Олимпией is a locative phrase ('on the sofa with Olympia') that functions as the predicate: 'he [will be] on the sofa with Olympia.'
segments.p2-5-g68: Проклятая is an adjectival past participle in the nominative feminine singular meaning 'cursed' or 'damned,' used here as a forceful exclamation modifying the name. The noun Олимпия follows in the nominative as the one being cursed; the phrase is an emphatic interjection.
segments.p2-5-g69: Она is the subject 'she.' Смеялась is the imperfective past tense of смеяться, here conveying 'laughed' with the sense of mocking; the imperfective stresses the ongoing/habitual or non-instantaneous character of the action. Раз after the verb here means 'once' (one time). Над + instrumental (над моим лицом) literally 'over my face' is used idiomatically to mean 'in my face / at me' — the preposition над with the instrumental indicates the target of the mockery. И links the two past actions; отказалась is the perfective past of отказаться meaning 'refused' or 'turned (me) down,' and от governs the genitive меня ('me'), so отказалась от меня = 'she refused me.' The aspect shift (imperfective смеялась → perfective отказалась) implies the mocking happened, and then she refused him.
segments.p2-5-g60: Ванька is a diminutive proper name (nominative) referring to the subject. Задергал is past tense, masculine singular, perfective with the prefix за- indicating the beginning or a series of short tugs (so “began tugging” / “gave a tug”); it governs an instrumental complement. Вожжами is the instrumental plural of вожжа, used to mark the means or instrument (“with/reins”), placed after the verb in normal SVO order.
segments.p2-5-g61: Как here introduces a temporal subordinate clause with the sense “as soon as / when,” not “how.” Войду is first-person singular, future simple of войти (perfective), meaning “I will enter” or “I enter (and complete the action).” Together they form a temporal clause: “When/as soon as I enter.”
segments.p2-5-g62: Так refers back to the preceding temporal clause and means “then/in that case.” И is a small particle here adding emphasis (colloquial: “indeed/just/even”), and дам is first-person singular future of дать (“I will give”). The object is omitted and understood from context (e.g. a slap).
segments.p2-5-g63: Надобно is an impersonal predicate meaning “is necessary,” followed by the enclitic question particle ли to form “is it necessary.” Сказать is the infinitive “to say,” completing the impersonal construction (“is it necessary to say”). Перед plus the instrumental (пощечиной) gives a temporal/positional sense “before the slap.” Несколько takes a genitive plural (слов) to mean “a few/some words.” В виде is a fixed expression meaning “in the form/by way of,” followed by предисловия in the genitive to indicate “of a preface.” The whole is a rhetorical question about whether a short preface is needed prior to the slap.
segments.p2-5-g64: Нет is a short exclamation/particle meaning “no” or a denial. As a standalone interjection it rejects or opposes the preceding idea; grammatically it functions as an independent negative response.
segments.p2-5-g1: Так вот оно is an emphatic introductory phrase: так (so/thus) + вот (here/there) draws attention, and оно (it) is the demonstrative subject. Word order (adverb + particle + pronoun) makes it an exclamation meaning roughly “so there it is.”
segments.p2-5-g2: This repeats the demonstrative (так вот оно) and adds наконец (finally) to time-mark the event; столкновенье-то is a noun 'collision/encounter' with the emphatic particle -то attached to столкновенье to stress it, followed by the preposition с governing the noun действительностью in the instrumental ("with reality").
segments.p2-5-g3: бормотал я is a simple past tense clause (мuttered I). The past masculine form бормотал precedes the pronoun я for colloquial or reported-speech effect; the dash marks the speech tag.
segments.p2-5-g4: сбегая is a present adverbial participle (деепричастие) meaning 'running down/descending' and indicates an action simultaneous to the main verb; стремглав is an adverb 'headlong'; с + лестницы uses с with the genitive to mean 'down/from the staircase.'
segments.p2-5-g5: Это is a demonstrative pronoun introducing or pointing to the thing being described ('this' or 'it'). Here it sets up a contrastive clause.
segments.p2-5-g6: знать is used here as a parenthetical particle (colloquial/idiomatic) meaning roughly 'you know' or 'as you may be aware' rather than the full lexical sense 'to know.'
segments.p2-5-g7: уж не combines the emphatic particle уж with negation не to strengthen the denial ('certainly not' / 'definitely not'); папа is the noun being negated (here understood as 'the Pope').
segments.p2-5-g8: оставляющий Рим и уезжающий в Бразилию are present active participles (деепричастные/причастные формы) modifying папа: оставляющий ('leaving') Рим and уезжающий ('departing') в Бразилию. They form a descriptive participial phrase in the nominative that specifies what kind of 'папа' is being denied.
segments.p2-5-g9: A repetition of the demonstrative это to introduce the second contrasting item; same function as the earlier Это.
segments.p2-5-g10: Again, знать is the parenthetical particle 'you know' / 'as it happens,' used colloquially to hedge or emphasize the statement rather than as the full verb 'to know.'
segments.p2-5-g11: уж не бал на озере Комо denies that the situation is some romanticized event: уж не (certainly not) + бал (a formal dance) + на озере Комо (on/at Lake Como). На governs озере (locative form), and Комо names the lake (Lake Como).
segments.p2-5-g12: Подлец ты is an exclamatory address with the noun 'scoundrel' placed before the pronoun ты for emphasis; there's no verb — a common, forceful vocative-style construction in colloquial speech.
segments.p2-5-g13: пронеслось is a past neuter form meaning 'flashed through' or 'swept through' (neuter because the impersonal 'it' is implied); в моей голове is a locative phrase 'in my head/mind' with моей in the genitive agreeing with голове.
segments.p2-5-g14: коли is an archaic/colloquial conjunction meaning 'if' or 'when' (here conditional 'if'); над этим uses над + instrumental to mean 'at/about/over this' in the sense 'to laugh at something' and смеешься is present indicative, 2nd person singular ('you laugh/are laughing'), with теперь 'now' marking time.
segments.p2-5-g46: Мы is the nominative plural personal pronoun 'we' and functions as the subject. Тронулись is the past tense, perfective, reflexive verb (тронуться) in 1st person plural (we) meaning 'to begin to move' or 'to set out'; the reflexive ending -ся marks intransitivity (to start moving oneself). Word order is subject + verb, a simple complete clause.
segments.p2-5-g47: Целый modifies вихрь and is a prepositive adjective meaning 'whole/complete' (an emphatic determiner). Вихрь is the nominative singular masculine subject 'whirl/whirlwind.' Кружился is the past imperfective, 3rd person singular of кружиться ('to whirl; to be spinning'), indicating an ongoing or repeated action; в моей голове is a prepositional phrase of location ('in my head'), with моя inflected to моей and голова to голове in the prepositional case.
segments.p2-5-g48: На коленях умолять is an infinitival phrase where на коленях ('on [their] knees') is an adverbial locative expression and умолять is the imperfective infinitive 'to implore/plead.' О моей дружбе uses the preposition о + prepositional (моей дружбе) to mark the object of pleading ('for/about my friendship'). After the dash, они не станут is a short clause with они (they) as subject, the negative particle не before the future perfective verb станут (3rd person plural of стать) meaning 'will not [do/become]' — here idiomatically 'will not go so far as to' or 'won't (do that).' The dash separates the action being refused from the refusal.
segments.p2-5-g49: Это is a demonstrative pronoun used as the copular subject/predicator ('it/this is'), followed by the noun мираж in the nominative acting as the predicate noun. Russian omits an explicit present-tense 'to be', so the juxtaposition of Это + noun reads as 'It is a mirage.'
segments.p2-5-g50: Пошлый is an adjective placed before the noun to qualify it ('tawdry/vulgar'), and мираж repeats the noun for emphasis; the comma continues the appositive series describing the same thing.
segments.p2-5-g51: Отвратительный is a standalone adjective in the nominative agreeing with the understood subject (the mirage) and continues the list of epithets; its position (isolated by commas) gives it emphatic, attributive force.
segments.p2-5-g52: Романтический и фантастический are two coordinated adjectives (connected by и 'and') in the nominative continuing the descriptive series; they agree implicitly with the noun 'mirage' and add contrasting qualities ('romantic and fantastic').
segments.p2-5-g53: Тот же is a fixed phrase where тот ('that') + же (emphatic particle) together mean 'the very same' or 'that same.' Бал is the noun 'ball/dance' in nominative, and на озере Комо is a prepositional phrase of place ('on Lake Como') with озере in the prepositional case and Комо as the place name (indeclinable here). The whole phrase specifies the particular event being recalled.
segments.p2-5-g70: Я is the 1st-person singular subject pronoun. Оттаскаю is the perfective future 1st-person singular of оттаскать (here written as оттаскаю) meaning 'I will haul/drag away' and takes a direct object (Олимпию). Олимпию is the accusative singular of the female name Олимпия, the direct object. За + accusative (за волосы) here indicates grabbing 'by' or 'by means of' (grabbing someone by their hair); волосы is plural accusative (collective 'hair').
segments.p2-5-g71: А here is a coordinating particle meaning roughly 'and' or contrasting 'but/and' linking the second action; it introduces the next object. Зверкова is the accusative (or vocative-like) form of the surname Зверкова (the woman named Zverkova) as the object. За уши repeats the construction за + accusative meaning 'by the ears' (literally 'by ears'), with уши plural accusative 'ears'. The exclamation mark makes the whole sentence an emphatic threat.
segments.p2-5-g72: Нет is a short negative/exclamatory particle meaning 'no' or 'not so' used here to interrupt or reverse the previous idea.
segments.p2-5-g73: Лучше is an adverbial comparative meaning 'better' or 'rather' proposing an alternative action. За одно ухо и за ухо are two repeated prepositional phrases with за + accusative used to indicate 'by one ear and by (the) ear' (instrumental/hold meaning). Проведу is the perfective future 1st-person singular of провести meaning 'I will lead/drag/escort' and takes его ('him') as its direct object. По + noun here (по всей комнате) means 'throughout/around' with всей комнате marking 'the whole room' (всей modifies комната); по governs the dative/locative form here to indicate movement across a space.
segments.p2-5-g74: Они is the 3rd-person plural subject pronoun 'they' referring to the people present; the comma sets it off for the following comment.
segments.p2-5-g75: Можно читать как modal construction: может быть is a two-word phrase functioning as 'maybe'/'it may be'. Здесь может is 3rd-person singular of мочь used for possibility and быть is the infinitive/verb 'to be/occur' forming the idiom 'may be/maybe'.
segments.p2-5-g76: Все начнут меня бить is a future-tense clause: все 'everyone/all' is the subject; начнут is 3rd-person plural future of начать 'will begin'; followed by the infinitive бить 'to beat/strike' and the object меня 'me'. И вытолкают is a second finite verb phrase (вытолкают is 3rd-person plural future of вытолкать) meaning 'they will push/expel (me)', coordinated by и 'and'. Both verbs are perfective futures describing subsequent actions.
segments.p2-5-g77: Это is the dummy pronoun 'this/it' referring to the previously stated possibility. Даже is an adverb meaning 'even' used for emphasis. Наверно (colloquial навéрно/наверняка) is an adverb meaning 'probably' or 'likely' — here it qualifies the speaker's assessment as probable.
segments.p2-5-g110: Ферфичкин is the subject in the nominative (a proper name). сказал is the past-tense, masculine, singular form of сказать and introduces reported speech or a quoted utterance. давеча is an adverb meaning 'recently/the other day' modifying сказал. вместо governs a noun in the genitive and means 'in place of; instead of'; hence департамента is genitive of департамент (required by вместо). The dash marks that лепартамент is the substituted (spoken) form — лепартамент is a joking or mispronounced form presented as the quoted word.
segments.p2-5-g111: Но is a coordinating conjunction 'but' adding contrast. где is the interrogative adverb 'where'. взять is an infinitive used here to ask where one can obtain something ('to get / to take'); the clause is elliptical (no explicit subject). пистолетов is genitive plural: after взять in this colloquial question the genitive plural is used partitive/indefinitely ('any pistols' / 'pistols (some)') rather than the accusative.
segments.p2-5-g112: Вздор is an interjectional noun used as an exclamation meaning 'nonsense' or 'rubbish.' It stands alone as the speaker's immediate dismissive reaction.
segments.p2-5-g113: Я is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I' (explicit though Russian often drops pronouns). возьму is the first-person singular future of взять (perfective) — 'I will take' (i.e. take in advance). вперед (variant spelling of вперёд) here means 'in advance' and governs a genitive that follows it; жалованья is the genitive of жалованье ('pay, salary'), so вперед жалованья = 'in advance on (my) salary.' и links the coordinate verb куплю, where куплю is first-person singular future (from купить) 'I will buy'; the object is omitted but is understood from the prior question (pistols).
segments.p2-5-g152:
This is an elliptical, rhetorically phrased question: 'А' is a contrastive conjunction/particle beginning the thought ('but'/'and yet'), 'не' is the negation that helps form the rhetorical question, 'лучше' is the comparative adverb 'better' (from хороший), and enclitic 'ли' turns the phrase into an interrogative.
The clause is intentionally unfinished (ellipsis in English) and implies a following infinitive or action.
segments.p2-5-g155: Same elliptical interrogative as above but with lowercase 'а' (continuation of thought). 'а' introduces a contrastive suggestion, 'не' negates, 'лучше' is the comparative 'better', and 'ли' is the enclitic question particle; the clause again lacks an explicit verb or infinitive that the speaker leaves unstated.
segments.p2-5-g158: 'Прямо' here is an adverb meaning 'straight' or 'directly' and functions as an intensifier; 'теперь' means 'now'; the particle 'же' is emphatic (reinforcing immediacy); 'домой' is a directional adverb 'home' (to/towards home). The verb ('to go') is omitted, so the phrase is a clipped interrogative suggestion: '(shall I) go straight home right now?'.
segments.p2-5-g159: 'О' is an exclamatory particle equivalent to English 'Oh'; 'боже' (vocative-like form of бог) addresses or invokes God in an exclamation; 'мой' is the possessive 'my' giving the idiomatic 'my God!'. Together they form a common exclamatory phrase.
segments.p2-5-g119: This is the Russian possessive construction with у + genitive: у меня нет literally 'at me there is not', meaning 'I do not have'. нет is a negative existential 'there is not'. знакомых is in the genitive plural as the thing that does not exist (acquaintances). The word order places the possession phrase up front for emphasis.
segments.p2-5-g122: A single-word exclamation (nominative) used as an interjection; colloquial and abrupt. It functions as a standalone utterance, equivalent to English 'Nonsense!' or 'Rubbish!'
segments.p2-5-g123: крикнул is the past tense, masculine, perfective verb 'cried/shouted'; the subject pronoun я follows the verb for slightly emphatic or colloquial effect ('I cried'). This is a straightforward past action reporting the speech.
segments.p2-5-g124: взвихриваясь is a deverbial adverbial participle (деепричастие) indicating a concurrent or attendant action — 'becoming agitated/whirling about'. ещё больше is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'even more' that modifies the degree of agitation.
segments.p2-5-g125: A repetition of the exclamation вздор as direct speech; same usage as the prior segment, functioning as the spoken content being reported.
segments.p2-5-g126: Первый встречный is an idiomatic noun phrase meaning 'the first person one meets' or 'the first passerby' (первый = ordinal) and встречный here conveys 'oncoming/passerby'. на улице is a prepositional phrase indicating location ('on the street').
segments.p2-5-g127: к которому is a dative relative pronoun 'to whom' linking back to 'первый встречный'; я is the subject; обращусь is the perfective verb 'обратиться' in the 1st person singular future 'I will turn/appeal to' — here used in the relative clause 'whom I shall/turn to'.
segments.p2-5-g128: обязан is the short-form adjective meaning 'obliged/required' and combines with the infinitive быть to express necessity: 'must be'. моим секундантом uses the instrumental case (моим ... секундантом) to denote the role 'my second' (секундант in the sense of a duel second/assistant). точно так же is a fixed comparative phrase 'exactly/just the same'.
segments.p2-5-g129: как introduces a comparison clause 'as/just as'. вытащить is the infinitive 'to pull out/rescue' functioning as the example action; из воды is 'out of the water' with из + genitive; утопающего is the present active participle in the accusative singular 'the (one) drowning' — together: 'to pull a drowning person out of the water.'
segments.p2-5-g114: А functions as a coordinating particle here—an abrupt 'and/what about' used to introduce another item; пороху is the noun порох in the genitive singular (porokhu) used elliptically to mean 'what about gunpowder' or 'the gunpowder?' with a pause indicated by the comma.
segments.p2-5-g115: А again introduces another item ('and/what about'); пуль is the genitive plural of пуля (puli) used elliptically to ask 'and the bullets?'—the genitive plural is common in Russian short interrogative fragments meaning 'what about X'.
segments.p2-5-g116: Это is the demonstrative 'this/that' used as a copular pronoun ('this is'); дело is a nominative predicate noun 'matter/business'; секунданта is in the genitive singular, marking possession or responsibility ('the business of the second'—i.e. the duel second's affair).
segments.p2-5-g117: И 'and' links this question to the previous remarks; как is the interrogative 'how' (manner/means); успеть is the perfective infinitive 'to manage (in time)'—used here in an impersonal interrogative construction ('how to manage'); все это is a unit meaning 'all this' (всё + demonstrative это); к + рассвету uses the preposition к with the dative to mean 'by (the time of) dawn'.
segments.p2-5-g118: И 'and' again connects to the prior questions; где is the interrogative 'where' (place); я is the pronoun 'I'; возьму is the future tense, 1st person singular of the perfective взять ('I will take/get/find'), indicating a single future action; секунданта is the accusative singular of секундант—the direct object meaning 'a second' (the person who acts as a duelist's second).
segments.p2-5-g105: Он is the nominative pronoun 'he' (subject). Даже is an adverbial particle adding emphasis ('even') placed before the verb. Вздрогнул is the perfective past tense of вздрогнуть, meaning a sudden, completed reflexive motion ('flinched/started'); и simply coordinates the two past verbs. Взмахнул is the perfective past of взмахнуть ('waved/brandished, gave a swing'); кнутом is the instrumental case of кнут and marks the instrument used ('with a whip'). The sequence is a normal Russian past narrative: subject — emphatic adverb — past verbs — instrument in instrumental case.
segments.p2-5-g106: Очень уж here is a colloquial double degree marker: очень 'very' + уж as an emphatic particle ('indeed/so' or 'really'); together they strengthen the following manner adverb. Дико is an adverb modifying the verb ('wildly'). The pronoun я is the nominative subject; крикнул is the perfective past of крикнуть ('shouted, cried out'). Word order (degree + manner + subject + verb) places strong emphasis on how the action was done.
segments.p2-5-g107: На рассвете is a prepositional time phrase: на + locative/old-style locative case рассвете — 'at dawn.' Деремся is the present-tense 1st-person-plural form of драться ('we fight'); in spoken Russian present tense can express a planned near-future action ('we'll fight') when announcing arrangements. The clause is a concise colloquial statement of plan.
segments.p2-5-g108: Это is the demonstrative pronoun 'this/it' used as a dummy subject. Уж here functions as 'already' or emphatic 'indeed' (stressing the settled nature of the matter). Решено is the short neuter past participle/adjective from решить used predicatively to mean 'decided' or 'settled' (impersonal passive-like construction: 'it is decided'). Together: 'This is already decided.'
segments.p2-5-g109: С + instrumental (с департаментом) means 'with the department' or 'as for the department' here indicating the object/target of the phrase. Кончено is the short-form past/adjectival result of кончить used impersonally to mean 'it's over/finished' (colloquial). The phrase literally 'with the department it is finished' = 'we're done with the department' or 'it's over as far as the department is concerned.'
segments.p2-5-g78: Пусть is an optative/imperative particle meaning 'let (someone)...' or 'may it be so'; as a single-word exclamation it gives permission or resignation ('let them'). It does not inflect and here stands alone as a full utterance.
segments.p2-5-g79: Все же is a concessive/emphatic phrase here ('after all' / 'still'); the main clause follows with subject я and past-tense perfective verb дал ('gave'). Первый is an ordinal modifying я (I was the first), and пощечину is the direct object in accusative (to give a slap). The colon signals that what follows will explain or restate this fact.
segments.p2-5-g80: A short nominative apposition: моя инициатива — an elliptical clause restating the previous fact ('my initiative'). Моя is the possessive adjective agreeing with инициатива (feminine nominative). The semicolon links this brief comment to the next clause.
segments.p2-5-g81: А по законам чести uses а ('but/and') to contrast, with по + dative (законам) meaning 'according to' or 'by (the) laws of honour'. The dash and the phrase это всё ('that's all') summarize the social consequence: 'by the laws of honour that's all.' Здесь это is a demonstrative pronoun pointing back to the previous idea.
segments.p2-5-g82: Он уже заклеймен is a short passive-state expression: заклеймен (short passive participle) = 'is branded/stigmatized', with уже = 'already'. The coordinate clause uses an instrumental means phrase никакими побоями ('by no beatings') with the perfective verb смоет ('will wash off') — смоет is future because the perfective infinitive is used; уж adds emphasis ('now/indeed'). Смыть ... с себя is an idiom 'wash off from oneself' meaning figuratively to remove a stain or dishonour.
segments.p2-5-g83: Кроме как is a set expression meaning 'except (only) by' or 'except through', and дуэлью is in the instrumental case indicating the means or manner ('by a duel'). This closes the conditional limitation on how the stain could be removed.
segments.p2-5-g84: Должен is a short-form predicative adjective used as a modal 'must/ought to'; with the auxiliary будет ('will') it expresses a future obligation: 'will have to'. Драться is the imperfective infinitive 'to fight', indicating the action required. Word order is Subject (Он) — predicate (должен будет) — infinitive.
segments.p2-5-g85: Да и is a colloquial connective meaning roughly 'and besides' or 'fine/after all' to introduce resignation; пусть + 3rd-person plural present (бьют) is the standard construction for permitting or conceding ('let them beat'). Бьют is present imperfective (ongoing/repeated action). Меня is the accusative object 'me', and теперь places the action in the present/now.
segments.p2-5-g86: Пусть is an imperative/optative particle (elliptical imperative) meaning 'let (them) do it' or 'go on'; it has no expressed subject here and relies on the previous context for who is meant. It functions as a dismissive permission/exclamation rather than a full clause.
segments.p2-5-g87: Неблагородные is the plural adjective used as a vocative-like address ('you ignoble ones'). It stands in the nominative plural but functions as a direct insulting appellation aimed at the people already referred to.
segments.p2-5-g88: Особенно is an adverb 'especially/above all' modifying the following predicate. Будет бить is the periphrastic future (будет + infinitive) meaning 'will beat' — here a simple future tense; the surname Трудолюбов follows the verb (inverted word order) and functions as the subject ('Trudolyubov will beat').
segments.p2-5-g89: Он is the pronoun 'he', referring back to Трудолюбов; такой is an intensifying demonstrative/adverb meaning 'so/such (as to convey degree)'; сильный is a descriptive adjective 'strong' (physical strength). Together: 'he is so strong.'
segments.p2-5-g90: Прицепится is the perfective reflexive future of прицепиться 'to latch on/attach oneself' (here 'he will latch on'). Сбоку is an adverbial of place 'from the side'. И links the two coordinated actions. Непременно is an adverb 'certainly/without fail'. За + волосы here expresses the manner of grabbing—'за волосы' = 'by the hair' (literally 'for/onto the hair'), with за marking the point of grip and волосы in the accusative as the thing seized.
segments.p2-5-g91: Наверно is a sentence-final adverb meaning 'probably' or 'I suppose' and signals the speaker's uncertainty about the precise detail just asserted.
segments.p2-5-g92: Но is a conjunction 'but/however' that contrasts with the previous remark; пусть again is the optative/imperative particle 'let them (do so)' used here to concede or dismiss. Together they form a concessive exclamation 'But let them...'.
segments.p2-5-g93: Пусть repeated as a standalone exclamation for emphasis — an imperative particle giving permission or dismissing concern ('let them!'). The repetition strengthens the speaker's resignation or defiance.
segments.p2-5-g94: Я is the first-person singular pronoun 'I'. На то is a prepositional phrase where на + 'то' means 'for that' or 'to that end/purpose'. Пошел is the past tense, masculine singular of пойти used idiomatically here: 'пошёл на то' means 'I went in for that' / 'I agreed to that' or 'I did that deliberately' — a completed action with nuance of having taken that course.
segments.p2-5-g143: Погоняй is an imperative form addressing a single driver; here it functions as a colloquial, energetic command meaning 'drive on' or 'get going.' Its placement at the start and the following comma mark a vocative address coming next.
segments.p2-5-g144: Извозчик is a noun in the nominative used as a direct address (Russian has no separate vocative), 'cabman' or 'hack driver'; the trailing comma marks it as the person being spoken to.
segments.p2-5-g145: This repeated Погоняй is the same imperative as the first, repeated for emphasis; the comma after it again prepares the following direct address.
segments.p2-5-g146: Шельмец is another nominative direct address, an insulting or teasing noun ('rogue/rascal') applied to the driver; its position between commas shows it's a vocative insertion.
segments.p2-5-g147: The final Погоняй, with an exclamation mark, closes the string of imperatives; repetition increases urgency and emotion in the speaker's command.
segments.p2-5-g148: Эх is an interjection (a sighy or exclaiming particle) that expresses surprise, resignation or emphasis; the following comma links it to the direct address.
segments.p2-5-g149: Барин is a nominative noun used as a direct address meaning 'master' or 'sir'—here it's a conventional polite (or ironic) form of address toward a gentleman or passenger.
segments.p2-5-g150: Проговорила is the past tense, feminine singular form of проговорить ('utter/pronounce/blurt out') and agrees with the feminine noun сила that follows; the verb-before-subject order here is a stylistic inversion emphasizing the act of speaking.
segments.p2-5-g151: Холод is the nominative subject 'cold'; вдруг is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying the verb; обдал is the past-tense (masculine) of обдать meaning here 'blew upon/struck' or 'swept over' and takes меня as its direct object—together they form 'a cold suddenly blew over me.'
segments.p2-5-g95: This whole clause is a single clause with a passive periphrastic future. 'Их' is the genitive pronoun 'their' modifying 'бараньи башки' (an insulting plural nominal phrase 'sheep(-)like heads' = 'stupid people'). 'принуждены будут' is the short participle 'принуждены' (passive: 'forced') combined with the auxiliary 'будут' to form 'will be forced'; 'же' is an emphatic particle adding insistence ('after all/indeed'). The infinitive 'раскусить' (perfective) is the action they will be forced to do ('to figure out/see through'), 'наконец' is an adverb 'finally', 'во всем этом' is a prepositional phrase 'in all this' (vo used before a vowel), and 'трагическое' is a substantivized adjective in the neuter used as the object ('the tragic' / 'the tragic aspect').
segments.p2-5-g96: 'Когда' introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). 'они будут тащить' uses the auxiliary 'будут' + imperfective infinitive 'тащить', giving a future continuous/ongoing sense 'will be dragging'. 'меня' is the accusative object 'me', and 'к дверям' uses 'к' + dative plural to indicate direction 'toward the doors'.
segments.p2-5-g97: Main clause: 'я закричу им' — 'я' (I) + 'закричу', a perfective future singular of 'закричать', which expresses a single, completed shout ('I will cry out/shout'). 'им' is the dative 'to them' (the indirect object receiving the shout).
segments.p2-5-g98: 'что' is the subordinating conjunction 'that', introducing the content of what he will shout.
segments.p2-5-g99: 'в сущности' is a parenthetical adverbial phrase meaning 'in essence' or 'essentially' (в + сущности, with 'сущности' = 'essence/core'), used here to soften or qualify the forthcoming statement.
segments.p2-5-g100: Reported clause: 'они не стоят моего одного мизинца' — 'они' (they) + negation 'не' + 'стоят'. Here 'стоят' (contextually from 'стоить') means 'are worth' rather than literal 'stand'. 'моего одного мизинца' is a genitive noun phrase 'of my one little finger' (моего = my, одного = one/single, мизинца = little finger), i.e. 'not worth a single one of my little fingers.'
segments.p2-5-g101: 'Погоняй' here is an imperative/interjection urging the driver to spur on the horses; it's an informal or colloquial command equivalent to 'drive on!' or 'go on!'.
segments.p2-5-g102: 'извозчик' is the vocative/address 'cabman' or 'hack driver' — the person being shouted to; Russian does not mark a special vocative form here, so the noun stands in direct address.
segments.p2-5-g103: Repetition of the imperative 'погоняй!' for emphasis; same informal command urging the horses/driver to hurry on.
segments.p2-5-g104: Simple past narration: 'закричал я на ваньку.' 'закричал' is the perfective past 'shouted/cried out', 'я' is the subject 'I' (postposed for stylistic emphasis), 'на' + accusative 'ваньку' indicates the target of the shout 'at Vanka' (colloquial diminutive of Ivan).
segments.p2-5-g130: Самые эксцентрические случаи is a superlative adjective phrase (самые + adjective + noun) in the nominative plural serving as the subject. должны быть допущены is a modal + passive construction: должен(ы) (modal 'ought to/must') + auxiliary быть + short passive participle допущены, yielding 'must be allowed/permitted.' Word order is standard: subject first, then the modal + passive predicate.
segments.p2-5-g131: Да functions as a colloquial linking/interjectory particle ('why'/'well'/'and') introducing the hypothetical; если + б is the conditional clause marker (если = 'if', б = conditional particle forming a subjunctive meaning 'were to'); я is the subject 'I'. самого даже директора is an emphatic accusative object: самого ('the very') + даже ('even') + директора (accusative) = 'even the director himself'. завтра is a time adverbial 'tomorrow'. попросил governs the accusative object and here takes в + role phrase (see next). в секунданты is в + plural noun used to indicate taking on the role 'as a second/into the position of a second.'
segments.p2-5-g132: то и тот is a resumptive/result clause opener: то ('then/so') + и ('even/also') + тот ('that one' = 'he'), giving 'then even he'. должен бы был согласиться stacks modal and conditional: должен ('ought to') + бы (conditional particle) + был (past auxiliary) + согласиться (infinitive) yields an emphatic conditional 'would have to agree' / 'would have to have agreed'. из одного рыцарского чувства uses из + genitive to express motive 'out of/from a single (one) chivalrous feeling'. и сохранить тайну links a second coordinated infinitive (сохранить) with the same modal meaning: 'and (would) keep the secret.'
segments.p2-5-g133: This is a nominal (verbless) sentence: given name + patronymic used together as an address or reference. Антоныч is an informal/familiar form of the patronymic (colloquial address). No verb is expressed; the phrase functions as a name or vocative.
segments.p2-5-g136: Дело в том is a fixed phrase meaning 'the point/the matter is that': дело (nominative 'matter/point') + в том (prepositional pronoun 'in that' used to introduce explanation). It sets up the explanatory subordinate clause that follows.
segments.p2-5-g137: что introduces the content clause ('that'). в ту же самую минуту is a time adverbial 'at that very same minute/moment' (в + demonstrative ту + emphatic же + самую + минуту). мне is the dative experiencer 'to me'. яснее и ярче are comparative/adverbial predicates ('clearer and more vivid') that will attach to the verb later; these comparatives are placed before the verb for emphasis.
segments.p2-5-g138: чем begins the comparison ('than'). кому бы то ни было is an idiomatic concessive phrase meaning 'to anyone whatever' (кому 'who/anyone' + бы + то ни было adding indefiniteness/emphasis). во всем мире is a prepositional phrase 'in the whole world.' Together the phrase reads 'than to anyone in the whole world.'
segments.p2-5-g139: представлялась is an imperfective past reflexive verb meaning 'presented itself/appeared' (here agreeing in form with the first coordinated noun). вся гнуснейшая нелепость моих предположений is the primary nominal subject phrase ('the whole most vile absurdity of my assumptions') and весь оборот медали is the second coordinated noun ('and the whole reverse/turn of the medal'). Note the verb is singular feminine (представлялась) agreeing with the first noun (нелепость) even though two nouns are coordinated—a common pattern in Russian.
segments.p2-5-g140: но is the conjunction 'but/however' placed alone here as a trailing, abrupt turn or interruption; it signals that something follows or that the thought is cut off (ellipsis).
segments.p2-5-g169: Они is the nominative plural pronoun 'they' (subject). Должны is a short-form predicative adjective used here as a modal: 'ought to / must' and agrees in number with они; it is followed by the infinitive. Смыть is a perfective infinitive 'to wash off / to erase' used figuratively here ('wipe away / remove'). Это is a demonstrative modifier in accusative/neuter form pointing to the following noun. Бесчестие is a neuter noun in accusative (same form as nominative) meaning 'dishonour' and is the direct object of смыть. The word order is subject — modal predicative — infinitive — object, producing an emphatic demand (exclamation).
segments.p2-5-g170: Погоняй is an imperative form (colloquial) telling someone to set off a chase or to drive/urge after something; the object is omitted but understood from context ('them' or 'after them'). The single-word exclamation functions as a command.
segments.p2-5-g171: А что is a conversational opener: А here works like 'but' or 'and' used to introduce a counter-hypothesis; что is the interrogative 'what' (used to start a rhetorical question 'But what...'). This fragment sets up the following conditional clause.
segments.p2-5-g172: Если introduces a conditional clause ('if'). Они is the subject 'they'. Меня is the accusative pronoun 'me' as the direct object. В is the preposition meaning 'into / to' introducing the destination. Часть here means 'section/unit' (destination, not merely 'a part'); together в часть = 'to the unit/section'. Отдадут is the 3rd-person-plural future form of the perfective verb отдать 'to hand over / consign,' so the clause asks 'if they should hand me over' — Russian frequently uses a future form in such hypothetical questions.
segments.p2-5-g173: Не is the negation 'not'. Посмеют is the 3rd-person-plural future of the perfective verb посметь 'to dare'; with не it yields the strong assertion 'they will not dare' (often translated 'they won't dare' or 'they wouldn't dare'). The short exclamation expresses confident refusal or defiance.
segments.p2-5-g160: Зачем is an interrogative adverb here used as a rhetorical exclamation ('why? / what for?'); the comma and repetition that follows mark emotional self-questioning rather than a real information request.
segments.p2-5-g161: This clause repeats the rhetorical зачем and adds temporal (вчера) and subject (я) with the past reflexive verb вызвался (past masculine singular + -ся) meaning 'volunteered/put myself forward'; на + accusative (на этот обед) expresses purpose/occasion ('for this dinner').
segments.p2-5-g162: Но here is the conjunction 'but/however' introducing contradiction; нет is an emphatic exclamation 'no' (strong negation) standing alone to reject the previous thought.
segments.p2-5-g163: Невозможно is an impersonal predicate meaning 'it is impossible' or 'impossible!' — Russian often omits a subject with such adjectives/adverbs functioning as the whole predicate.
segments.p2-5-g164: А is a contrastive conjunction ('and/but'); прогулка is the noun 'walk/stroll' and is followed by the enclitic particle то (≃ 'indeed/after all') which adds emphasis; три часа gives the duration 'three hours'; от... до... ('from... to...') marks the route or span 'from the table to the stove'. The clause is a surprised question.
segments.p2-5-g165: Нет is again an exclamation 'No' negating or rejecting the previous possibility; it's a standalone interjection here.
segments.p2-5-g166: Они is the nominative plural pronoun 'they' — the subject being strongly repeated for emphasis.
segments.p2-5-g167: The repeated они intensifies the speaker's insistence; repetition is a common rhetorical device in Russian to stress determination.
segments.p2-5-g168: А introduces contrast ('but'); не кто другой literally 'not anyone else' (не + кто + другой) excludes all others; должны is a modal/predicative plural 'must/ought to' agreeing with они; расплатиться is perfective reflexive 'to settle accounts / pay up'; со мною uses the preposition со + instrumental to mean 'with me' (i.e. settle up with me); за + accusative (за эту прогулку) means 'for this walk' — the object of recompense.
segments.p2-5-g240: This is a simple past-tense clause with the proper name subject Ванька (diminutive of Ivan) followed by two adverbial modifiers and the past-tense verb. 'С изумлением' is an instrumental noun phrase functioning adverbially ('with astonishment'). 'И вздыхая' is a деепричастие (adverbial participle) meaning 'and sighing' that describes a concurrent action. 'Смотрел' is the masculine singular past of смотреть ('looked/was looking'), and 'на меня' is the prepositional phrase marking the object of the look ('at me').
segments.p2-5-g241: 'Что' here is the interrogative 'what'. The construction 'было + infinitive' is impersonal and idiomatic in Russian to express a past necessity or lack of options ('there was nothing to be done' / 'what was one to do?'). 'Делать' is the infinitive 'to do' and remains unchanged; the sentence asks rhetorically what could be done.
segments.p2-5-g242: 'И' is simply the coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'Туда' is an adverb meaning 'to there / to that place/point'. The impersonal phrase 'было нельзя' expresses impossibility in the past ('one could not / it was not possible'). After the dash 'выходил вздор' uses выходил (past imperfective of выйти) with вздор (nonsense) to mean 'it would turn out to be nonsense' — a colloquial way to say the result would be absurd.
segments.p2-5-g243: Again 'и' = 'and'. 'Оставить' is the perfective infinitive 'to leave/abandon'. 'Дела' is the accusative plural of дело here meaning 'the matter/affair' (what needed doing). 'Нельзя' repeats the impersonal modal 'cannot / it is not allowed/possible', so the clause means 'and one couldn't abandon the matter.' Note the lack of an explicit subject — the construction is impersonal.
segments.p2-5-g244: 'Потому что' is the causal conjunction 'because'. 'Уж' is a particle here adding emphasis/urgency ('already/at once/indeed'). 'Тут' means 'here' or 'at that point/then'. 'Выйдет' is the future tense of выйти and is used impersonally: 'it will come out / it will be revealed / it will turn out' — meaning the matter would become known or an awkward result would occur if he left it.
segments.p2-5-g247: An exclamatory vocative form from господь used as an interjection: an appeal or exclamation 'Lord!' or 'My God!'. It stands alone to express surprise, distress, or strong feeling.
segments.p2-5-g206: Через пятнадцать лет is a temporal expression 'in fifteen years' with через + a numeral phrase indicating time ahead. Я потащусь is the 1st person singular future of the perfective reflexive verb потащиться, here meaning to drag myself along / trudge (implying reluctance); note the reflexive ending -сь. За ним means 'after/behind him' (i.e. in pursuit of or following him). В рубище describes appearance/clothing ('in rags') with в + noun indicating being clothed in/inside that garment.
segments.p2-5-g207: Нищим is in the instrumental form and functions adverbially/nominally to indicate role or state 'as a beggar' or 'being poor'; Russian uses the instrumental to express 'in the capacity of' or 'as'.
segments.p2-5-g208: Когда introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. Меня is the accusative object 'me' (the person affected). Выпустят is 3rd person plural future (perfective) 'they will let out/release' with an implied agent 'they'. Из shows separation 'from/out of' and острога is the genitive of острог 'stockade/wooden prison', completing 'when they release me from the stockade.'
segments.p2-5-g209: Я отыщу is 1st person singular future of a perfective verb 'отыскать/отыщу' meaning 'I will find' or 'I will come upon' (emphatic perfective). Его is the direct object 'him'. Где-нибудь is the adverbial combination where где 'where' plus particle-нибудь makes it indefinite 'somewhere'. В губернском городе uses в + prepositional (губернском городе) to indicate location 'in a provincial/gubernial town', with the adjective губернском agreeing with городе.
segments.p2-5-g210: Он будет is the simple future construction ('he will be'). Женат and счастлив are short-form adjectival predicates: женат ('married' used of a man) and счастлив ('happy'). Short-form adjectives after быть describe a state/result; и simply links the two predicates.
segments.p2-5-g211: У него is the possessive construction literally 'at him' meaning 'he has' (here indicating possession). Будет is future 'there will be/will have' used existentially to express future possession. Взрослая дочь is the noun phrase 'an adult daughter' (взрослая is an adjective in nominative agreeing with дочь). Together: 'He will have an adult daughter.'
segments.p2-5-g196: Разумеется is an introductory adverb meaning 'of course' or 'naturally' used to mark the speaker's attitude; it usually appears at the sentence front and is set off by a comma.
segments.p2-5-g197: После этого is a temporal prepositional phrase ('after this/that') modifying the following clause. Всё (subject) + уже (adverb 'already') + кончено (short form past participle of кончить used impersonally) yields a state: 'everything is already finished/over.' Кончено here functions as a predicative adjective/participial adjective indicating a completed state.
segments.p2-5-g198: Департамент is the nominative subject. Исчез is a past-tense verb (masculine singular) meaning 'disappeared/vanished.' С лица земли is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ('from the face of the earth')—с + accusative of лицо + родительный земли—used here to emphasize complete disappearance.
segments.p2-5-g199: Меня is the accusative object 'me' (no explicit subject). Схватят is 3rd person plural future perfective (or simple future) indicating a single, completed action 'they will seize/arrest me.' The agent is unspecified and implied by the plural verb.
segments.p2-5-g200: Меня (accusative) + будут судить uses the auxiliary будут (3rd person plural future of быть) + infinitive судить to form the future tense of an imperfective verb: 'they will judge/try me.' This construction emphasizes the forthcoming process of being tried.
segments.p2-5-g201: Меня (accusative) + выгонят is 3rd person plural future perfective meaning 'they will expel/dismiss me.' Из службы is a prepositional phrase with из + genitive служба meaning 'from the service' (i.e., dismissed from official/civil/military service).
segments.p2-5-g202: Посадят is 3rd person plural future perfective of посадить used here in the sense 'to imprison/incarcerate.' В + острог marks the destination/place ('into a stockade/prison'). The subject is again omitted and implied by the verb form.
segments.p2-5-g203: Пошлют is 3rd person plural future of послать; in this context it specifically means 'they will send (into exile),' not merely 'dispatch.' В Сибирь indicates the destination 'to Siberia' often understood as exile.
segments.p2-5-g204: На поселение is a prepositional phrase giving the purpose/destination of the previous verb: на + поселение means 'to (a) settlement'—here understood as a penal settlement. На indicates direction/purpose ('to/for') rather than location.
segments.p2-5-g205: Нужды is in the genitive singular after нет (the standard construction for 'there is no ...'), so 'no need/necessity.' Нет is the negation/existential 'there is no.' Together they form the emphatic exclamation 'There is no need!'
segments.p2-5-g174: The noun Скандала is in the genitive and precedes the verb for emphasis; Russian uses the genitive with побояться to mark what one fears. Побоятся is 3rd person plural future (from побояться, perfective), meaning ‘will be afraid’ or ‘will fear’. The simple two-word sentence omits an explicit subject (they) because the verb form makes the 3rd-person plural subject clear.
segments.p2-5-g175: А is an adversative/conjunctive particle here, functioning like English 'but'. Что is an interrogative particle ('what') forming the colloquial opener 'А что,' = 'But what (if)...' — it doesn't stand alone as a full clause but signals a hypothetical question to follow.
segments.p2-5-g176: Если introduces a conditional clause ('if'). Зверков is the subject (a surname) followed by the motive phrase из презренья ('out of contempt'), where из + genitive indicates motive. Откажется is 3rd person singular future of отказаться (perfective), and the verb takes от + genitive (от дуэли) to mark the thing being refused — 'refuse the duel.' Word order (motive between subject and verb) is normal flexible Russian placement for emphasis.
segments.p2-5-g177: Это is the demonstrative pronoun 'this/that' referring back to the preceding idea. Даже is an adverb of emphasis ('even' / intensifier). Наверно is an adverb expressing probability ('probably' / 'likely'); together the phrase means something like 'That's even likely' or 'That's most likely.'
segments.p2-5-g178: Но is the adversative conjunction 'but.' Я is the first-person subject. Докажу is 1st person singular future of доказать (perfective) — a promise to produce a completed proof: 'I will prove.' Им is the dative plural pronoun 'to them' (indirect object). Тогда is an adverb 'then / in that case' specifying the time or consequence. The clause follows a typical Subject–Verb–IndirectObject–Adverb order.
segments.p2-5-g181: Я is the subject 'I.' Брошусь is 1st person singular future of броситься (perfective), literally 'I will throw myself,' used idiomatically as 'I will rush' or 'I'll fling myself' and implies movement toward a place. Тогда is 'then / in that case.' На + accusative (на почтовый двор) marks motion toward the location; почтовый двор is a compound meaning the coach/stagecoach yard (a place where mail coaches gather).
segments.p2-5-g182: Когда introduces a temporal clause ('when'). Он is the subject 'he.' Будет + imperfective infinitive уезжать forms a periphrastic future progressive ('will be leaving'), stressing the ongoing action at that time. Завтра is the time adverb 'tomorrow' modifying the verb.
segments.p2-5-g183: Схвачу is 1st person singular future (perfective) of схватить — a sudden, decisive action: 'I will grab.' Его is the accusative pronoun 'him' as the direct object. За + noun here (за ногу) expresses grabbing by a body part ('by the leg') using the accusative after за in this collocation.
segments.p2-5-g184: Сорву is 1st person singular future (perfective) of сорвать — 'I will tear off' (a completed action). С + genitive (с него) means 'off him' or 'from him' (removing something that he is wearing). Шинель is the object in the accusative: a greatcoat (a military-style overcoat), the item to be torn off.
segments.p2-5-g185: Когда opens a second temporal clause ('when/as'). Он is 'he.' Будет + imperfective влезать forms the future progressive 'will be climbing/trying to get in,' indicating an action in progress at that moment. В + accusative (в повозку) marks movement 'into' the vehicle (повозка = cart/coach), so the whole means 'as he is climbing into the carriage.'
segments.p2-5-g214: Я is the nominative personal pronoun 'I' (subject). Скажу is the first-person singular, future tense of the perfective verb сказать, meaning 'I will say' (a single, completed utterance); the colon introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.p2-5-g215: Смотри is the imperative singular of смотреть, used here as the direct command 'Look!' addressed to someone; the comma after it sets up the vocative address that follows.
segments.p2-5-g216: Изверг is a noun used as a direct address (Russian has no separate vocative, so the nominative form is used) and functions as an insulting vocative: 'wretch' or 'scoundrel'—an abusive name for the person being spoken to.
segments.p2-5-g217: Смотри (imperative again) is followed by the preposition на + accusative (смотреть на) meaning 'look at' or 'look toward'. Мои is the plural possessive 'my' agreeing with щеки (feminine plural). Ввалившиеся is a past participle/participial adjective from ввалиться meaning 'caved in / sunken' and modifies щеки ('sunken cheeks'); щеки is the plural noun 'cheeks'. И introduces the second object with на + accusative: на мое рубище — на (preposition used here in the sense 'at/toward') + мое (possessive neuter singular) + рубище (neuter noun, 'rags/coarse garment').
segments.p2-5-g218: Я is the subject 'I'. Потерял is the masculine past tense of потерять (perfective) meaning 'lost' (the narrator is male, hence masculine agreement). Всё (все) is the pronoun 'everything' (the broad object lost). The dash begins an enumerated apposition (a list of what 'everything' consists of); карьеру is the accusative singular of карьера 'career' as the first item in the list.
segments.p2-5-g219: Счастье is a neuter noun 'happiness' (here in the accusative form identical to nominative) and is the next item in the enumerated list of things lost.
segments.p2-5-g220: Искусство is a neuter noun 'art' (or 'the arts'), another coordinated item in the accusative-list; its form is unchanged from nominative in this context.
segments.p2-5-g221: Науку is the accusative singular of наука 'science' or 'learning'—again a coordinated direct object in the list of losses.
segments.p2-5-g222: Любимую is the feminine accusative singular of любимый 'beloved' modifying женщину (agreeing in gender, number and case). Женщину is the accusative singular of женщина 'woman' (here 'the woman I loved' or 'my beloved woman').
segments.p2-5-g223: И is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the final comment. Всё here repeats 'everything' (the pronoun). Из + за together form the compound preposition из-за 'because of / on account of'; из is the first element (literally 'out of/from'), за is the second element (here functioning within the compound), and they require the genitive (тебя) — thus из-за тебя = 'because of you' (тебя is the genitive/accusative form of ты used after из-за).
segments.p2-5-g224: Вот is a presentational particle meaning 'here/there is' used to point something out. Пистолеты is the nominative plural 'pistols' (the thing being pointed out); the construction 'Вот + nominative' is a common way to present or show something.
segments.p2-5-g225: Я is the subject 'I'. Пришел is the masculine past tense of прийти 'came/arrived'. When followed by an infinitive (разрядить), пришел expresses purpose: 'came in order to ...'. Разрядить is the infinitive 'to discharge' (perfective), here acting as the purpose of coming. Свой is the reflexive/subject-linked possessive 'my' (referring back to the subject). Пистолет is the accusative singular 'pistol' (object of разрядить). The final и ('and') left hanging is stylistic—it suggests an unfinished thought or that something more follows, creating an abrupt stop.
segments.p2-5-g228: и is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this utterance to the preceding speech. прощаю is 1st person singular present (or present-tense perfective performative) of прощать/прощать (here 'I forgive' as a speech act). тебя is the accusative pronoun 'you' and is the direct object of прощаю.
segments.p2-5-g229: Тут here means 'at that moment/then' (an adverb placing the action in time). The subject я follows the adverb for natural Russian word order. выстрелю is the 1st person singular future (perfective) of выстрелить — 'I will fire/shot' — and на воздух (literally 'onto the air') is an idiomatic accusative phrase meaning 'into the air'.
segments.p2-5-g230: и ('and') joins the second clause. обо is the preposition об in the form обо used before местоимения for euphony; обо мне = 'about me'. The paired particles ни ... ни introduce a negative correlative; здесь ни слуху ни духу is an idiom meaning 'not a whisper/not a trace' (i.e. 'no news').
segments.p2-5-g233: Я is the subject 'I'. было here is the particle/auxiliary that softens or qualifies the past action (common in colloquial past constructions: 'я было заплакал' = 'I was on the point of crying' or 'I nearly cried'). даже is an adverb 'even' adding emphasis. заплакал is the past tense perfective of заплакать 'to begin to cry/burst into tears.'
segments.p2-5-g234: хотя is the concessive conjunction 'although'. совершенно and точно are adverbial intensifiers 'completely/absolutely' and 'exactly' modifying знал. знал is the past imperfective 'knew' (indicating his awareness). в это же самое мгновение is a time phrase 'at that very same instant' — в + demonstrative pronouns with же and самое provide strong emphasis on the exact moment.
segments.p2-5-g235: что introduces the content clause 'that ...'. все это = 'all this' (весь/все used as a generalizing pronoun). из + name/ title (из Сильвио, из "Маскарада" Лермонтова) indicates origin/source — 'from Silvio' and 'from Lermontov's "Masquerade"' (Лермонтова is genitive after the title).
segments.p2-5-g236: И is the coordinating 'and' linking to the previous remark. вдруг is the adverb 'suddenly'. мне стало is an impersonal construction literally 'it became to me' used to express a sudden feeling ('I felt'). ужасно is an intensifying adverb ('terribly/very') modifying the predicative стыдно ('ashamed/embarrassed').
segments.p2-5-g237: до того is a fixed intensifying phrase meaning 'to such a degree/so (much)'. Here it reinforces стыдно: 'so ashamed.' Grammatically до takes the genitive demonstrative того in this idiom.
segments.p2-5-g238: что introduces the result clause 'that ...'. я is the subject and остановил is the past tense perfective 'stopped' with лошадь as its direct object ('I stopped the horse'). This describes the consequential action caused by the shame.
segments.p2-5-g239: вылез (perfective past) means 'climbed out' (from a vehicle). из саней = 'out of the sleigh' (из + genitive). и links the next action; стал here = 'stood' / 'took a stand' (past of стать used to indicate assuming a position). в снег среди улицы is a spatial phrase 'into/standing in the snow in the middle of the street' — среди улицы uses среди + genitive to mean 'in the midst of the street'.
segments.p2-5-g186: Subject 'Я' (I) begins the clause; 'зубами' is instrumental plural ('with [my] teeth') indicating the means. The verb 'вцеплюсь' is the perfective, reflexive form of 'вцепиться' in the 1st person singular future ('I will grip/ cling on'), and it takes a dative person 'ему' ('to him') plus a directional/target phrase 'в руку' (preposition 'в' + accusative 'руку') showing the body part targeted. Word order emphasises the instrument (зубами) before the verb for vividness.
segments.p2-5-g187: Parallel short clause with subject 'я' and the perfective future verb 'укушу' (1st person singular, 'I will bite'), followed by the pronoun object 'его' (him). The terse, simple S-V-O order makes this a punchy, emphatic follow-up to the previous clause.
segments.p2-5-g188: 'Смотрите' is the 2nd person plural imperative of 'смотреть' ('Look' or 'Watch'), addressing an audience; 'все' here is a vocative/appeal meaning 'everyone' (all of you). The two-word phrase is a direct call to the crowd.
segments.p2-5-g189: The exclamatory construction 'до чего можно довести...' literally 'to what (extent) one can bring/drive ...' uses 'до' ('up to/to') plus interrogative 'чего' ('what' in exclamative sense) with impersonal 'можно' ('one can/it is possible') and the infinitive 'довести' (perfective, 'to drive/bring to a state'). The noun phrase 'отчаянного человека' is the object (accusative) meaning 'a desperate man'; together the clause expresses astonishment at the extent to which a desperate person may be driven.
segments.p2-5-g190: 'Пусть' + indicative (here 'он бьет') is a concessive imperative meaning 'let him ...' or 'even if he ...'; 'он' is the subject 'he'; 'бьет' is present tense 3rd person singular of 'бить' ('strike/hit'); 'меня' is the accusative object 'me'; 'в голову' ('in/at the head') uses 'в' with accusative to mark the place on the body being struck. The clause concedes or proposes the action will happen.
segments.p2-5-g191: Contrastive 'а' links this short verbless clause to the previous one ('and/but'), 'все' here means 'all' (the rest of them), 'они' is the pronoun 'they', and 'сзади' is an adverb 'behind'/'at the back'. Russian often omits the copula 'are' in the present, so this is understood as 'and all of them are behind (him/them)'.
segments.p2-5-g192: Simple clause: subject 'Я' (I), indirect object 'всей публике' (dative: 'to the whole audience') and the perfective future verb 'закричу' ('I will cry out/shout'). The dative marks the addressee of the shout; the perfective verb conveys a single, loud exclamation.
segments.p2-5-g193: Imperative 'Смотрите' again, addressing the listeners ('Look'/'Behold') — a direct vocative call introducing what follows.
segments.p2-5-g194: 'Вот' is a demonstrative particle ('here/is/there') used to point something out; 'молодой щенок' is a nominative noun phrase ('a young puppy') serving as the thing pointed to. The order 'вот + NP' is a common way to present or expose someone to the audience.
segments.p2-5-g195: Relative clause introduced by 'который' ('who/which') modifying 'щенок'. 'Едет' is the 3rd person singular present of 'ехать' ('go/ride/leave'), here in the sense of 'is off/going' (motion toward some end) and it is followed by the infinitive 'пленять' ('to captivate/take captive') forming the action the puppy is undertaking. 'Черкешенок' is the object of 'пленять' (a Circassian girl), while the phrase 'с моим плевком на лице' uses 'с' + instrumental 'моим плевком' ('with my spit') to indicate accompaniment/manner and 'на лице' ('on the face') to mark location. The construction paints a vivid image: he goes to captivate a girl while carrying the mark/insult of the speaker's spit on his face.
segments.p2-5-g248: This whole clause is an exclamatory rhetorical question. Как же combines the interrogative 'как' (how) with the emphatic particle 'же' to heighten astonishment; 'это' is the demonstrative 'this/it'; 'можно' is an impersonal predicate meaning 'is it possible' (so the construction literally reads 'how (ever) is it possible to leave this'); 'оставить' is the infinitive governed by the impersonal modal 'можно'.
segments.p2-5-g249: 'И' here functions as a linking/exclamatory particle (roughly 'and' or 'even') that adds force to the complaint; 'после' is the preposition 'after' and takes the genitive; 'таких' is the genitive plural of 'такой' ('such'); 'обид' is the genitive plural of 'обида' ('insult, offense'), so the phrase literally means 'and after such insults' as an outraged interjection.
segments.p2-5-g250: A one-word exclamation: 'Нет' is the simple negative interjection 'No!' used here as an emphatic denial or protest. It stands alone as a reply/exclamation and is not functioning as an existential 'there is no...'.
segments.p2-5-g251: вскликнул is a perfective past tense verb (masculine singular) meaning 'exclaimed' or 'cried out'; the verb precedes the pronoun 'я' (I), which is a normal word order for a short authorial parenthetical ('I cried'). The comma marks this as a reporting tag framing the speech.
segments.p2-5-g252: снова is an adverb 'again'; кидаясь is a деепричастие (adverbial participle/gerund) from 'кидаться' meaning 'throwing oneself' or 'hurriedly flinging oneself' and indicates a simultaneous or attendant action ('while/again flinging myself'); в + сани (в with accusative) denotes motion 'into the sleigh'. The phrase modifies the main verb 'вскликнул' (describes how he cried).
segments.p2-5-g253: это is the demonstrative pronoun 'this/it'; предназначено is the short-form passive/adjectival past (neuter) of 'предназначить', used impersonally to mean 'is destined/ordained' or 'was meant to be'. Together they form an impersonal predication: 'this is destined.'
segments.p2-5-g254: A brief exclamatory copular statement: 'это' ('it/this') followed by the noun 'рок' ('fate, doom')—no verb is necessary in Russian for this equative clause. The phrase 'это рок!' functions as an emphatic exclamation: 'it's fate!'
segments.p2-5-g255: Погоняй is an imperative form, a colloquial order addressed to the driver or to urge the horses — literally 'drive!' or 'get them going!'. As an isolated vocative-like exhortation it repeats for emphasis and links directly to the following identical imperative.
segments.p2-5-g256: The repeated погоняй is the same imperative again, used for emphasis (a common spoken intensification). Repetition strengthens the command and prepares for the directional adverb that follows.
segments.p2-5-g257: Туда is a directional adverb meaning 'to there'/'that way' and follows the imperatives to indicate the desired direction for the horses or carriage.
segments.p2-5-g258: И is a simple coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this action to what came before. В + noun in the prepositional (в нетерпении) forms a set phrase 'in impatience'; я is the subject; ударил is past tense, masculine singular, perfective 'struck/ hit'; кулаком is an instrumental noun 'with a fist' indicating the means; извозчика is the direct object in genitive (colloquial accusative form) 'the cabman'; в шею is a prepositional/accusative phrase indicating the target 'in/at the neck'. The whole is a single past action, literal and sequential.
segments.p2-5-g259: Да here is an exclamatory particle/introductory interjection (colloquial, roughly 'oh' or 'come now') used to express surprise or protest; что ты is an abrupt, colloquial 'what are you (doing)?' or 'what's the matter with you?' — что is the interrogative/exclamatory 'what' and ты is the informal singular 'you'. The phrase is elliptical and spoken.
segments.p2-5-g260: Чего is the colloquial/genitive form of что used in questions/exclamations meaning 'what/why' (here functioning as an interrogative/exclamatory particle), and дерешься is the present-tense reflexive verb meaning 'are you fighting/struggling' or 'making a fuss'. Together they form the spoken question 'what are you getting into?/why are you fighting?'.
segments.p2-5-g261: Закричал is past tense, masculine singular, perfective 'cried out' or 'shouted'; мужичонка is a diminutive/pejorative noun 'little man' or 'peasant fellow' used contemptuously. Together they present the speaker and his shouted exclamation.
segments.p2-5-g262: Стегая is a gerund (деепричастие) from стегать, meaning 'whipping' or 'flogging' (here of the horse); it indicates an attendant action performed by the speaker while he shouted.
segments.p2-5-g263: Однако ж is a colloquial combination: однако 'however' or 'still' and the particle ж adding emphasis — together they soften the reproach and add a conversational tone ('but still' / 'however, mind you'). It interrupts the clause that names the object being whipped.
segments.p2-5-g264: Клячу is the accusative/demonstrative reference to the animal 'the nag' (кляча) — a contemptuous word for a worn-out horse. It's the direct object of the gerund/verb стегая (he was whipping the nag).
segments.p2-5-g265: Так что introduces a result clause 'so that/with the result that'; та is a demonstrative pronoun 'that one' (referring back to the mare/horse); начала is past tense 'began' (feminine agreeing with та); лягаться is an imperfective reflexive verb 'to kick (with hind legs)' describing the horse's reaction; задними ногами is an instrumental/ablative plural phrase 'with (her) hind legs', specifying how she kicked. Together the clause states the result of the whipping.
segments.p2-5-g281: Наконец is an adverb meaning “at last/finally,” placed sentence-initial for emphasis. Мы is the nominative plural pronoun “we,” the subject. Подъехали is the past tense, plural form of the perfective verb подъехать, meaning a completed action “drove up/approached (by vehicle)” or simply “arrived” — together: “At last we arrived/pulled up.”
segments.p2-5-g282: Я is the first-person singular subject pronoun “I.” Выскочил is the past masculine singular of the perfective verb выскочить, meaning “bolted/out” or “jumped out” (sudden, completed action). Почти modifies the following phrase as an adverb (“almost”), and без + памяти uses the preposition без with the genitive памя́ти to mean “without (conscious) memory” — i.e. “almost unconscious/senseless.”
segments.p2-5-g283: Взбежал is past masculine singular of взбежать, a perfective verb meaning “ran up” (a quick ascent). По + dative plural (ступенькам) expresses movement along/up the steps here: “up the steps.” И links the two actions; начал + infinitive (стучать) marks the start of a repeated/ongoing action: “began to knock.” В with accusative дверь marks the target of the knocking (“at/on the door”), and руками и ногами is an instrumental phrase “with hands and feet.”
segments.p2-5-g284: Особенно is an adverb meaning “especially/most of all,” placed to emphasize the following noun. Ноги is the nominative plural noun “legs,” here topicalized (short phrase + comma) to signal that the legs, in particular, are being described.
segments.p2-5-g285: В with the locative/ prepositional plural коленках indicates location/part of the body: “at/in the knees” or “in the knees.” The plural form коленках refers to the knee areas (colloquial plural used for parts of the body).
segments.p2-5-g286: У меня is a possessive/experiencer construction literally “at me,” used to say something is happening to a body part (“my legs”). Указывает possession/experience. Ужасно is an adverbial intensifier (“terribly/very”), and слабели is the past plural imperfective of слабеть, meaning “grew weak” or “were weakening” (imperfective indicates an ongoing/gradual state in the past and agrees with ноги).
segments.p2-5-g287: Как-то is a colloquial particle-combination meaning roughly “somehow/for some reason” or “rather” (here modifying the following adverb). Скоро is an adverb “soon/quickly,” and отворили is the past plural of отворить (perfective), a somewhat colloquial verb for “opened” — together: “they opened (for me) rather quickly/somehow quickly.” The semicolon links this to the following remark.
segments.p2-5-g288: Точно is an adverb meaning “exactly/clearly” (here: “clearly / as if certainly”). Знали is the past plural of знать meaning “knew/were aware.” О introduces the object of that awareness (о моем приезде = “about my arrival”), with моем in the prepositional to agree with приезде. Приезде (prepositional of приезд) means “arrival/visit.”
segments.p2-5-g303: Где is the interrogative adverb 'where' asking about place. Же is an emphatic particle adding impatience or insistence (often translated as 'then'/'on earth'); it doesn't change syntax but strengthens the question. Они is the nominative plural pronoun 'they' (the subject); Russian omits a copula in present questions, so the three elements form the simple question 'Where (же) they?'.
segments.p2-5-g304: Спросил is the past tense, masculine, perfective form of спросить 'to ask' (here simple past: 'asked'). Я follows the verb for emphasis or stylistic inversion (common in Russian narrative), but is the nominative subject 'I'. Кого + то form the indefinite object 'кого-то' = 'someone'; кого is the accusative of кто, and the particle то cliticizes to make the indefinite pronoun. The word order (verb–subject–object) gives a slightly abrupt, reported-speech tone.
segments.p2-5-g305: Но is the coordinating conjunction 'but/however', linking this statement to the prior question/expectation. Они is the nominative plural pronoun 'they' (the subject); the comma sets it off as the topic.
segments.p2-5-g306: Разумеется is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'of course' or 'naturally', inserted to mark the speaker's assumption as obvious; it doesn't affect the verb morphology.
segments.p2-5-g307: Уже is the adverb 'already'. Успели is the past tense, plural form of успеть (perfective) meaning 'managed to' or 'had time to' — it implies they succeeded in doing it before now. Разойтись is the perfective infinitive 'to disperse/part', and together успели разойтись means 'managed to disperse'. Word order (already + managed + infinitive) is typical Russian for this nuance.
segments.p2-5-g310: Передо мной is a locative expression 'in front of me' (перед with the variant передо used before a pronoun), where мной is the instrumental of 'I' and marks the reference point. Стояла is the past feminine singular of стоять ('stood'), agreeing with the feminine noun личность; the inversion (locative phrase + verb + noun) is a normal literary order. Личность here means 'a person' or 'individual' (a certain person).
